blocks.text:
If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following rule:

$$dz =  rac{dz}{dx}dx +  rac{dz}{dy}dy$$.
The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$, $y$, and $z$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21. Change in position on a surface.
blocks.description: The figure illustrates how a change in position on a surface (represented by point Q) is based on the rate of change in x and y, leading to a corresponding change in z. It visually conveys the relationship between the variables in a differential context.

blocks.text:
Thus:

$$	ext{change in } z = 	ext{change in } x 	imes  rac{dz}{dx} + 	ext{change in } y 	imes  rac{dz}{dy}$$.

By this rule, for a point $P$ at $(x, y)$, an increase in $x$ and $y$ can be measured by the product of the change in $x$ multiplied by the rate at which $z$ is changing with respect to $x$; and this fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21).
